meta.title: Props and emits vs v-model for a Vue custom input API
meta.summary: A Vue custom form component needs to send value changes back to its parent. Would you design it around explicit props and emits or expose a v-model API?

scenario: You are building a Vue 3 billing form with a reusable `PriceRangePicker` component. The parent page owns the committed filter value and wants the picker to behave like a normal form field inside larger forms. The picker lets users adjust a min and max amount, validates basic bounds, and sends the chosen range back to the parent. One teammate wants to expose a standard `v-model` API so the component feels like a normal input. Another teammate wants to keep the public API fully explicit with named props and emits such as `:value`, `@change`, `@apply`, and `@reset`. In the interview, the panel asks which direction you would defend.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Design the component around v-model
options.summary: Fits best when the component behaves like a form control with one primary editable value that should sync naturally with the parent.
whenItWins: The component's main job is to edit one committed value and send updates back to the parent like a normal input field.
You want the API to feel consistent with other form controls so parent components can wire it up predictably.
Most of the interaction is still about one editable value rather than a long list of distinct commands.
watchOutFor: v-model becomes weaker when the component is really a small workflow with draft, apply, cancel, preview, and multiple event semantics that deserve explicit names.
A weak answer picks v-model only because it is shorter, without checking whether the component really behaves like one form field.

options.label: Use explicit props and emits
options.summary: Fits best when the public API needs several distinct interaction events and not just one two-way value contract.
whenItWins: The component has multiple meaningful events such as open, close, apply, cancel, clear, or preview that should stay explicit.
The parent should distinguish between draft interaction and committed value changes rather than treat everything as one model update.
The component behaves more like a mini workflow or control surface than like a simple input.
watchOutFor: An explicit API can become noisy if the component really is just a form control with one primary value and standard update behavior.
A weak answer avoids v-model on principle even when the component should simply plug into forms like any other input.

decisionMatrix.title: One primary editable value
decisionMatrix.prompt: The component behaves like a normal form field and mainly needs to keep one value in sync with the parent.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because the component's public contract is essentially 'edit this value'.
Still possible, but now you should explain why explicit event naming adds clarity instead of just extra ceremony around a form-control pattern.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: A reusable range picker should plug into forms as one value editor, while basic validation still stays within the field.

cells.note: This is the cleanest answer because the component behaves like a custom input with one primary committed value.
Possible, but harder to defend if the explicit API mostly recreates a form-control contract that v-model already expresses well.

decisionMatrix.title: Component acts like a mini workflow
decisionMatrix.prompt: The component has draft editing, apply versus cancel semantics, preview state, and several distinct interaction events the parent should understand separately.

cells.note: You can still use it for the committed value, but the answer weakens if the whole interaction surface gets flattened into a generic update channel.
This becomes easier to defend because the component API is now describing several behaviors, not just one model value.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is this really a form control or more of a mini workflow?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. v-model is strongest when the component behaves like an input, while explicit props and emits become more valuable when the component exposes richer behavior.

evaluationDimensions.title: What should the parent understand from the API?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should care about whether the parent mainly needs a value contract or needs to distinguish several different events and intentions.

evaluationDimensions.title: How naturally does this component fit into larger forms?
evaluationDimensions.description: If the component is meant to plug into many form flows, a familiar input-style contract can reduce friction.

evaluationDimensions.title: Do interaction events carry different meanings?
evaluationDimensions.description: When draft changes, apply, cancel, reset, and preview all mean different things, flattening them into one value update can hide important intent.

evaluationDimensions.title: Are you adding API surface the component does not need?
evaluationDimensions.description: A balanced answer should avoid both extremes: forcing v-model onto a workflow component or inventing many explicit events for a component that is really just a field.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean v-model because the prompt describes a custom form field with one primary committed value
strongAnswer.summary: This range picker is meant to plug into forms and behave like an input that edits one value owned by the parent. That makes v-model the easier answer to defend for the exact prompt.
reasoning: The key clue is component role. The parent owns one committed range value, and the picker's main job is to edit that value the way a custom input would.
That makes `v-model` a strong fit because the API communicates the same contract the rest of the form already expects: here is the current value, and here is how it updates.

I would move back toward explicit props and emits when the component stops acting like one field and starts acting like a mini workflow with apply, cancel, preview, or other event semantics that the parent should understand separately.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would expose the range picker as a `v-model`-driven custom input and keep explicit extra emits only for truly separate events. I would choose a more explicit props-and-emits API if the component evolved into a richer interaction workflow rather than a straightforward value editor.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not keep everything explicit with props and emits so the API is clearer?
interviewerPushback.answer: I would do that if the component had several distinct behaviors the parent needed to reason about separately. In this prompt, though, the primary contract is still 'edit this value', so v-model expresses the intent more directly and fits better with larger form usage.

interviewerPushback.question: When would explicit props and emits clearly be the better answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: When the component has meaningful event semantics beyond one value update, such as draft versus apply, cancel, preview, or reset, and the parent needs to respond differently to each one.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the picker grew into a panel with draft state, explicit apply and cancel buttons, and richer event semantics, I would move toward a more explicit props-and-emits contract or combine v-model with a few carefully named extra events.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use v-model because that is the Vue way.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds generic and teaches no boundary for when an explicit event API is actually the better design.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use v-model because the component is a form field and the parent mainly needs the current value.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a sharper explanation of component role and when richer event semantics justify explicit props and emits.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would design the range picker around `v-model` because it behaves like a custom form field with one primary committed value owned by the parent. I want the parent to use it the same way it uses other inputs, instead of recreating an explicit props-and-events contract for what is still mostly one value editor. I would switch toward a more explicit API if the component grew into a mini workflow with apply, cancel, preview, or other event semantics that the parent should respond to separately.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it starts from the role of the component, explains why input-style ergonomics matter here, and still gives a clear boundary for when explicit props and emits become the better contract.

answerFramework: Start by asking whether the component is mainly a value editor or a richer interaction workflow.
Check whether the parent mostly needs one model value or several distinct event meanings.
Choose v-model or explicit props and emits based on that contract.
Explain the cost of the other option, either flattening important events or overdesigning a simple form field.
End with a simple rule of thumb, for example: v-model for custom inputs with one primary value, explicit props and emits for components with richer event semantics.
antiPatterns: Using v-model only because it is shorter.
Avoiding v-model on principle even when the component is clearly a form control.
Flattening apply, cancel, preview, and reset semantics into one generic model update.
Designing a large explicit event API for a component that is really just editing one value.
